rows.title: Patents Act 1977: United Kingdom primary statute governing patent rights and procedure
rows.summary:
The Patents Act 1977 is the principal statute of the United Kingdom governing the grant, validity, ownership, infringement, and licensing of patents, enacted by the Parliament of the United Kingdom and given Royal Assent on 29 July 1977.

On 29 July 1977, the Parliament of the United Kingdom enacted the Patents Act 1977 (c. 37), which came into force in stages, with the main operative provisions taking effect on 1 June 1978. The Act replaced and largely superseded the Patents Act 1949 and was designed to give effect to the United Kingdom's obligations under the European Patent Convention (EPC) 1973 and the Patent Co-operation Treaty (PCT) 1970.

The Act establishes the substantive conditions for patentability, providing under section 1 that an invention is patentable if it is new, involves an inventive step, is capable of industrial application, and does not fall within specified excluded categories. Section 1(2) sets out those exclusions, which include discoveries, scientific theories, mathematical methods, mental acts, and computer programs as such. The Act confers on the patent holder, under section 60, the exclusive right to exploit the invention and defines acts constituting infringement, including making, disposing of, using, importing, or keeping a patented product without consent. The Act also provides, under sections 39 to 43, rules governing the ownership of employee inventions, setting out circumstances in which an invention made by an employee may belong to the employer or, alternatively, to the employee, and providing for compensation to employees where an employer holds a patent of outstanding benefit.

The Comptroller-General of Patents, operating within the Intellectual Property Office (IPO), is assigned administrative and adjudicative functions under the Act, including the examination and grant of patent applications and the resolution of certain disputes. The Act has been amended on multiple occasions, notably by the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 and the Intellectual Property Act 2014, the latter inserting provisions on opinions and reviews conducted by the IPO under sections 74A and 74B. The Act applies to the United Kingdom as a whole and extends to granted European patents (UK) by virtue of section 77, treating them as if they were patents granted under the Act itself.

rows.title: FCA Confirms Second Cohort of Eight Firms for AI Live Testing Programme
rows.summary:
The Financial Conduct Authority has selected eight firms, including Barclays, Experian, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), and UBS, to participate in the second cohort of its AI Live Testing programme, operating under existing FCA regulatory frameworks rather than new AI-specific rules.

On 21 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority announced at UK FinTech Week that the second cohort of its AI Live Testing initiative had commenced testing, with applications having opened in January 2026. No financial penalties are associated with this announcement. The FCA confirmed it is working with technical partner Advai, a London-based specialist in automated AI assurance, to deliver the programme. The full list of participating firms comprises Aereve, Coadjute, Barclays, Experian, GoCardless, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), UBS, and Palindrome.

The FCA states that the initiative is designed to help firms explore questions around risk management and live monitoring to support responsible AI deployment for consumers and markets. The authority notes that applications reflect a diverse range of AI models, spanning agentic AI, small language models, and neurosymbolic AI. The FCA finds that use cases under testing include AI-enabled targeted support for investments, credit score insights for consumers, agentic payments, anti-money laundering detection, and Know Your Customer processes, covering both customer-facing and business-to-business applications.

The FCA confirms that testing will conclude by the end of 2026, with an evaluation report to be published in the first quarter of 2027. The authority also states it intends to publish a good and poor practice report for AI in financial services later in 2026 to assist firms in the adoption of the technology. Separately, the FCA's Innovation Insights report, published alongside the announcement, finds that applications to the FCA's Regulatory Sandbox and Innovation Pathways increased by 49 per cent on the previous year. The FCA notes that its approach to AI relies on existing regulatory frameworks, avoiding the introduction of additional AI-specific regulation, a position the authority set out in its response to the Prime Minister's letter of January 2025.

rows.title: FCA Consults on Perimeter Guidance for UK Cryptoasset Regime Ahead of October 2027 Implementation
rows.summary:
The Financial Conduct Authority has launched a consultation, CP26-13, on perimeter guidance clarifying which cryptoasset activities will fall within the scope of the United Kingdom's forthcoming regulatory regime, established under The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Cryptoassets) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/102), made by Parliament on 4 February 2026.

On 15 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published consultation paper CP26-13, inviting feedback on its interpretation of five regulated cryptoasset activities: issuing qualifying stablecoin, operating trading platforms, dealing and arranging deals in qualifying cryptoassets, safeguarding cryptoassets, and staking. The FCA states the consultation closes on 3 June 2026, with a final policy statement due in autumn 2026.

The FCA notes that the full cryptoasset regulatory regime is due to come into force in October 2027, with policy statements on the substantive rules to be published in summer 2026. The FCA states that the authorisation gateway opens on 30 September 2026, at which point firms will be able to begin applying for authorisation under the new regime. The FCA finds that the perimeter guidance consultation complements the series of rule-making consultations already conducted, which addressed stablecoin issuance and custody (CP25/14), prudential requirements (CP25/15, CP25/42), application of the FCA Handbook (CP25/25, CP26/4), regulated cryptoasset activities (CP25/40), and admissions, disclosures and market abuse (CP25/41).

The FCA determines that, until the new regime comes into force, cryptoassets remain largely unregulated in the United Kingdom except for financial promotions and financial crime purposes. The FCA notes that further consultations are planned later in 2026, covering decentralised finance guidance, operational resilience guidance for firms using distributed ledger technology, and updates to the Financial Crime Guide relevant to cryptoasset firms. To support prospective applicants ahead of the gateway opening, the FCA states it is hosting a series of authorisation-focused webinars, including sessions on anti-money laundering regulations and the Senior Managers and Certification Regime.

rows.title: NCSC CEO Warns of 'Perfect Storm' for UK Cyber Security Amid AI Advances and Geopolitical Tensions
rows.summary:
The UK National Cyber Security Centre has stated that the United Kingdom faces a convergence of rapid technological change and geopolitical tensions creating a 'perfect storm' for cyber security, with the NCSC finding that the majority of nationally significant incidents now originate from nation-state actors.

On 22 April 2026, Dr Richard Horne, CEO of the National Cyber Security Centre, part of signals intelligence agency GCHQ, delivered a speech at the CYBERUK conference in Glasgow in which the NCSC set out its assessment of the current and emerging threat landscape facing the United Kingdom.

The NCSC finds that the number of incidents it handles remains 'fairly steady', but notes a material shift in their origin. The authority states that the majority of nationally significant incidents it is currently handling originate directly or indirectly from nation states. The NCSC further states that Russia is applying lessons learnt on the battlefield during the illegal invasion of Ukraine and moving them beyond the battlefield, and that cyber operations are now integral to conflict globally. The authority characterises cyberspace as existing in a state 'between peace and war', describing the cyber domain as constituting 'the home front'.

On the question of technological change, the NCSC states that frontier AI is rapidly enabling the discovery and exploitation of existing vulnerabilities at scale, illustrating how quickly it will expose where fundamentals of cyber security remain unaddressed. The authority finds that the definition of cyber security is expanding alongside the technology landscape, and that future cyber security will be required to cover robotics, autonomous systems, and technology physically integrated with the human body, all of which the NCSC describes as 'way beyond' how the discipline was understood a decade ago. The NCSC states that organisations failing to treat their technology base as core to their prosperity 'are no longer just naïve but are failing to grasp the reality of today's world'. The authority calls for a cultural shift within organisations, stating that cyber security is the responsibility of everyone, whether they sit on the Board or the IT help desk, and that cyber security is part of their mission.

rows.title: FCA Publishes Open Finance Roadmap, Targeting Regulatory Framework by End of 2027
rows.summary:
The Financial Conduct Authority has published a roadmap setting out its vision for open finance in the United Kingdom, prioritising expanded consumer and business control over financial data and committing to develop a regulatory framework in collaboration with HM Treasury by the end of 2027.

On 14 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published its open finance roadmap, outlining a programme to enable consumers and businesses to share financial data securely with a range of financial services providers. No fines or penalties are associated with this publication. The roadmap identifies potential applications across mortgages, investments, savings, pensions and credit access for small and medium-sized enterprises.

The FCA states that open finance will give financial services firms a more complete picture of consumers' and businesses' finances, enabling more personalised services, more competitive pricing and stronger fraud protection. The authority identifies SME credit access and mortgage management as priority areas for early exploration. To develop practical use cases, the FCA finds that engagement with industry, consumer groups and fellow regulators will be conducted during 2026 through the FCA's Smart Data Accelerator and its PRISM, Prioritisation and Real-world Insights Selection Matrix, Taskforce.

The roadmap notes that the FCA will consult on a proposed long-term regulatory framework for open banking before the end of 2026, distinct from the broader open finance framework that the authority is required to develop with HM Treasury by the end of 2027. The FCA states that firms will be supported to introduce open finance products earlier where they already have access to relevant data and appropriate permissions are in place. Research by Open Banking Limited and EY, cited in the roadmap's accompanying notes, estimates that the combined economic impact of open banking and open finance could reach £7.4 billion per year within five years, with full adoption potentially delivering £43 billion annually. The FCA does not adopt these figures as regulatory determinations but references them to illustrate the scale of the opportunity the roadmap is intended to address. Open banking currently has approximately 17 million users in the United Kingdom, representing close to one in three adults.

rows.title: ICO Publishes Updated Automated Decision-Making Guidance Reflecting Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Changes
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has updated its detailed guidance on automated decision-making and profiling to reflect amendments to the UK GDPR introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, consolidating obligations under the newly inserted articles 22A to 22D.

On 31 March 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office published a revised version of its automated decision-making guidance, incorporating changes to the UK GDPR resulting from the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025. The ICO states that the previous guidance remains available as a PDF and will be withdrawn once updated guidance is finalised following a concurrent consultation.

The ICO notes that the updated guidance addresses how organisations can determine whether their processing falls within the scope of the UK GDPR's article 22A provisions, which govern solely automated decisions with significant effects. The ICO states that automated decision-making, referred to throughout as ADM, is processing where a decision is based solely on automated processing, including profiling, with no meaningful human involvement, and produces a legal or similarly significant effect on a person, described in the UK GDPR as a significant decision (Art. 22A UK GDPR). The ICO clarifies that the term ADM provisions refers collectively to articles 22A to 22D of the UK GDPR.

The ICO states that the updated guidance clarifies when restrictions apply to an organisation's ability to carry out ADM and what conditions are required to be satisfied in those cases. The ICO notes that a new section has been added addressing the safeguards organisations are required to put in place and the rights individuals hold in relation to ADM that affects them. The ICO further notes that the guidance is aimed at data protection officers, compliance professionals and technical leads with oversight of ADM systems, including those deploying in-house tools or solutions provided by external vendors. The ICO states that this guidance will inform a statutory code of practice on artificial intelligence and ADM that the ICO will develop. The ICO notes that the guidance is specific to the UK GDPR ADM provisions and does not address part 3 or part 4 of the Data Protection Act 2018, which govern law enforcement and intelligence services processing respectively.

rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on Cloud-Hosted SCADA for Operational Technology Organisations
rows.summary:
The UK National Cyber Security Centre has published guidance on cloud-hosted Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems, setting out three critical assessment areas for operational technology organisations considering cloud migration.

On 24 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) published new guidance addressing the migration of SCADA solutions to cloud environments by operational technology (OT) organisations. The guidance carries no associated fine or enforcement action and does not reference a specific legislative basis, operating instead as technical and risk-management guidance directed at OT operators, including critical national infrastructure (CNI) operators.

The NCSC notes that, through engagement with industry, it has observed a clear shift in attitudes towards using cloud platforms for industrial applications. Where cloud adoption was previously dismissed by many OT organisations due to perceived risks, the NCSC finds that organisations in this sector are increasingly exploring cloud-based solutions. The NCSC states that any migration decision is required to be informed by each organisation's unique risk profile and specific technical requirements.

The NCSC identifies three critical areas that OT organisations are required to assess before deciding on a SCADA cloud migration: understanding business drivers and cloud opportunities; organisational readiness; and technology and cloud solution suitability. The guidance further identifies particular challenges facing OT organisations, including reliance on legacy equipment, on-premises solutions, and monolithic software packages, all of which the NCSC finds can complicate the path to secure cloud implementation. The NCSC notes that securing a cloud platform is a problem shared with information technology functions and states that the new guidance is intended to be used alongside its existing cloud security guidance collection and its using-cloud-services-securely guidance. The guidance was developed with input from industry bodies, CNI operators, and vendors within the SCADA community.

rows.title: ICO Places PECR Guide Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations under review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force in the United Kingdom.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations is subject to review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into law on that date. The ICO notes that the guidance may be subject to change and directs organisations to its Plans for New and Updated Guidance page for information on which guidance will be updated and when.

The ICO states that the guide is intended for organisations that send electronic marketing messages by phone, fax, email or text, use cookies, or provide electronic communication services to the public. The guide explains how to apply the Regulations through practical examples and answers to frequently asked questions. The ICO confirms that, pending the review, the existing guidance remains the current reference point for compliance with the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations.

The ICO's announcement reflects the broader legislative change introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which the authority identifies as the basis for placing existing PECR guidance under review. The ICO has not specified a timetable for the completion of that review but indicates that updates will be communicated through its dedicated guidance plans page. The ICO has not announced any amendments to the substantive obligations under the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations in connection with this review notice.

rows.title: ICO Places Children's Code Guidance Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Children's code guidance and associated resources under formal review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on 19 June 2025, with the ICO noting that existing materials may be subject to change.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Children's code guidance, which covers the age-appropriate design code of practice for online services likely to be accessed by children, is under review as a consequence of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 entering into law on that date. The ICO notes that no financial penalty is associated with this announcement.

The ICO states that the Children's code sets out fifteen standards with which providers of information society services likely to be accessed by children, including apps, online games, and social media sites, are required to conform. The ICO notes that the guidance applies to organisations of all sizes and across all sectors. The ICO states that resources available under the existing framework include a self-assessment risk toolkit enabling organisations to assess how both the UK GDPR and the Children's code apply to their digital services, Data Protection Impact Assessment templates, and sector-specific guidance covering edtech, the digital news industry, and games designers.

The ICO further states that its published Children's Code Strategy, together with a progress update issued in March 2025, sets out the authority's enforcement priorities and actions taken to date in relation to children's privacy online. The ICO's Commissioner's opinion on age assurance details how the Commissioner expects businesses to meet the code's age-appropriate application standard and sets out expectations for age assurance data protection compliance. The ICO also notes that it has co-signed a joint statement on a common international approach to age assurance, establishing shared principles across signatory regulators. The ICO states that its plans for new and updated guidance page is the reference point for information on which guidance will be updated and the timeline for those updates.

rows.title: ICO Publishes AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit Version 1.1
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published version 1.1 of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, providing a practical spreadsheet-based resource for organisations processing personal data through AI systems under the UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released an updated iteration of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, available for download as a Microsoft Excel file. The publication coincides with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its broader AI and data protection guidance is currently under review in light of that legislation and may be subject to change, with updates to be signalled via the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the toolkit is designed to provide practical support to organisations seeking to reduce risks to individuals' rights and freedoms arising from their own AI systems. The resource is framed as supplementary material sitting alongside the ICO's existing guidance on AI and data protection, which the ICO states addresses obligations under the UK GDPR including requirements relating to lawful basis, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and accountability (Art. 5, Art. 6, Art. 24 UK GDPR). The ICO presents the toolkit as a means of helping organisations work through data protection risk considerations in a structured, documented format consistent with the accountability principle.

The ICO confirms it is no longer seeking case study submissions from organisations in connection with the toolkit, contrary to an earlier call for contributions referenced in accompanying guidance materials. The toolkit is offered as a downloadable spreadsheet compatible with Microsoft Excel or equivalent programmes, and the ICO notes that organisations on networks that restrict access to video platforms such as Vimeo may be unable to view associated instructional video content and are advised to use an alternative device. The ICO states that no specific compliance deadline or enforcement context is attached to the toolkit's publication.

rows.title: ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on AI and Data Protection, Adding New Chapters on Fairness and Transparency
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published updated guidance on artificial intelligence and data protection under the UK GDPR, restructuring existing content and introducing new chapters on fairness, transparency, and lawfulness in AI systems.

On 15 March 2023, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released a revised version of its Guidance on AI and Data Protection, with the document remaining subject to further review following the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025. The ICO states that the guidance carries no financial penalty and imposes no fines; it is a compliance guidance instrument addressing obligations under the UK GDPR.

The ICO states that the update was issued in response to requests from UK industry for clarification on fairness requirements in AI, and that it delivers on a commitment under the ICO25 strategic plan to help organisations adopt new technologies whilst protecting individuals and vulnerable groups. The ICO finds that the guidance has been restructured around data protection principles, which provides a more editorially coherent and operationally sustainable framework given the pace of technological development.

The ICO notes that the revised guidance introduces several new and reorganised chapters. The ICO states that a standalone chapter on transparency sets out how the transparency principle (Art. 5(1)(a) UK GDPR) applies to AI, with detailed explainability content retained within the ICO's separate Explaining Decisions Made with AI product. The ICO notes that a new chapter on lawfulness includes added content on AI-generated inferences, affinity groups, and special category data (Art. 9 UK GDPR). The ICO states that it also introduces a dedicated fairness chapter covering the distinction between data protection fairness, algorithmic fairness, bias, and discrimination, as well as technical approaches to bias mitigation and the relationship between automated decision-making safeguards and fairness considerations (Art. 22 UK GDPR). The ICO notes that a new Annex A addresses fairness across the full AI lifecycle, from problem formulation through to decommissioning, identifying sources of bias arising from underlying assumptions, target variable selection, and reliance on quantifiable proxies. The ICO states that statistical accuracy content, previously located within the lawfulness and fairness chapter, has been relocated to a dedicated accuracy chapter reflecting its closer alignment with the accuracy principle (Art. 5(1)(d) UK GDPR). The ICO notes the guidance will continue to be updated to reflect forthcoming regulatory reform and the UK government's pro-innovation approach to AI regulation.

rows.title: ICO Issues Guidance on Personal Data Use in Political Campaigning Under UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published guidance on the use of personal data in political campaigning, setting out compliance expectations for controllers under the UK General Data Protection Regulation, the Data Protection Act 2018 and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (PECR).

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published its finalised guidance for the use of personal data in political campaigning, following a consultation launched in October 2019 on a draft framework code of practice. The ICO notes that the guidance is currently under review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into force on the same date, and may be subject to change. The ICO states that no fines are associated with this guidance publication.

The ICO states that the guidance is directed at controllers processing personal data for political campaigning purposes, defined as activity in support of, or against, a political party, a referendum campaign, a candidate standing for election, or the success or failure of a recall petition. The ICO confirms this includes registered political parties, candidates, referendum campaigners, non-party campaigners and recall petition campaigners as defined under the Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000 (ss. 23, 88, 105), the Representation of the People Act 1983 (s. 118A), and the Recall of MPs Act 2015 (Schedule 3, Part 5). The ICO notes that the guidance does not apply to internal party processes such as leadership elections.

The ICO finds that political campaigning has become increasingly sophisticated through the use of digital technologies and commercial marketing techniques, and that the often invisible nature of these techniques risks undermining public trust in the democratic process. The ICO states that the guidance does not introduce any new obligations or responsibilities beyond those already imposed by existing data protection and electronic marketing law, but is intended to provide clarity and practical advice to ensure that all organisations involved in political campaigning handle personal data in a manner compliant with the UK GDPR, the DPA 2018 and PECR, regardless of the method of communication or future technological developments.

rows.title: ICO Publishes Sample DPIA for Children's Mobile Gaming Apps Under Age-Appropriate Design Code
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published a sample Data Protection Impact Assessment for a fictional mobile gaming application aimed at children in the United Kingdom, intended to illustrate good practice compliance with Standard 2 of the Children's Code (Age-Appropriate Design Code) under UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published the sample DPIA, coinciding with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its guidance, including this resource, is under review and may be subject to change as a result of that legislation, with updates to be tracked on the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the sample DPIA is adapted from its own DPIA template and is intended to help companies developing mobile gaming apps for children in the UK understand and apply Standard 2 of the Children's Code, which the ICO notes requires Information Society Services likely to be accessed by children under the age of 18 to carry out a DPIA (Art. 35 UK GDPR). The document uses a fictional product, the Cooking Numbers mobile app, developed by the controller named as The Mobile Game Company, based on a product specification commissioned from Fundamentally Games for demonstration purposes only. The ICO confirms that the fictional product will not be developed into an actual game.

The ICO states that the sample follows the process set out in its DPIA guidance and the Children's Code, and recommends reading it alongside the code's dedicated DPIA guidance and the criteria for an acceptable DPIA established in European guidelines. The ICO further notes that its design guidance, which includes gaming-specific worked examples of data privacy moments and age-appropriate mindsets developed from the Fundamentally Games product specification, is available to assist practitioners in identifying what data is being processed and any associated risks. The authority invites feedback and recommendations for improvements to the sample DPIA by email.
